text: You have power over your mind, not outside events. Realize this, and you will find strength.
Men are disturbed not by things, but by the views which they take of things.
The unexamined life is not worth living.
I think, therefore I am.
That which does not kill us makes us stronger.
Act only according to that maxim whereby you can, at the same time, will that it should become a universal law.
All things excellent are as difficult as they are rare.
Repeating information right before you forget it strengthens memory far more than repeating it right after you learn it. This is called the spacing effect.
The brain tends to remember unfinished tasks better than completed ones, a pattern known as the Zeigarnik effect.
We suffer more often in imagination than in reality.
Never let the future disturb you. You will meet it, if you have to, with the same weapons of reason which today arm you against the present.
Repeated exposure to something tends to increase our liking for it, even without conscious awareness. This is the mere-exposure effect.
Waste no more time arguing about what a good man should be. Be one.
People tend to value something more highly once they have had a hand in building it, a bias known as the IKEA effect.
Negative experiences tend to affect us more strongly than positive ones of equal intensity, an asymmetry called negativity bias.
The best revenge is to be unlike him who performed the injury.
Most newly learned information is lost within the first day unless it is reviewed, according to Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve.
If it is not right, do not do it; if it is not true, do not say it.
Holding two contradictory beliefs at once creates mental discomfort that people are motivated to resolve, a state called cognitive dissonance.
People tend to overestimate how much others notice their mistakes and appearance, a bias known as the spotlight effect.
Very little is needed to make a happy life; it is all within yourself, in your way of thinking.
A single positive or negative trait can color our entire impression of a person, a pattern known as the halo effect.
Confine yourself to the present.
When we're anxious about a decision, adding one more piece of information often makes us feel more confident without actually making the decision more accurate.
People tend to conform to group opinions even when they privately believe the group is wrong, simply to avoid social friction.
Loss is nothing else but change, and change is Nature's delight.
Being told to consciously suppress a thought often makes that thought resurface more insistently later.
How much more grievous are the consequences of anger than the causes of it.
We tend to remember the first and last items on a list better than the ones in the middle.
Vividly imagining a future event in detail can make us more confident it will happen, even without new evidence.
Everything we hear is an opinion, not a fact. Everything we see is a perspective, not the truth.
People who write about stressful experiences for a few minutes a day tend to show measurable improvements in physical health over time.
The universe is change; our life is what our thoughts make it.
The way a question is phrased can change how people answer it, even when the underlying facts are identical.
Recalling a memory can subtly change it each time, a process called reconsolidation.
First say to yourself what you would be; and then do what you have to do.
People consistently rate their own driving ability as above average, a statistical impossibility for the group as a whole.
No man is free who is not master of himself.
Physical warmth can subtly increase feelings of interpersonal warmth toward other people, and vice versa.
People tend to attribute their own failures to circumstances but attribute others' failures to character.
Only the educated are free.
Choices become harder to make, not easier, once we're offered too many options.
Wealth consists not in having great possessions, but in having few wants.
Doing someone a small favor makes them more likely to return the favor later, sometimes even years afterward.
People tend to value what they already own more than they would pay to acquire the same item new.
He is a wise man who does not grieve for the things which he has not, but rejoices for those which he has.
Making a public commitment to a goal makes people more likely to follow through with it than making the same commitment privately.
It is not that we have a short time to live, but that we waste a lot of it.
We tend to see patterns and faces in random or ambiguous shapes, a tendency called pareidolia.
Groups often reach riskier decisions collectively than any individual member would reach alone.
Difficulties strengthen the mind, as labor does the body.
Physical pain and social rejection activate overlapping regions of the brain.
As long as you live, keep learning how to live.
The simple act of writing down a goal measurably increases the odds of achieving it.
Sleep deprivation impairs the brain's ability to regulate emotional reactions before it impairs conscious reasoning.
Every new beginning comes from some other beginning's end.
When people believe a pill is expensive, they often report it working better than when told it's cheap, even if it's the same pill.
Multitasking doesn't make us faster; it makes each task take longer because of the cost of switching attention.
He who is brave is free.
We consistently underestimate how long a task will take, even when we've made the same mistake on a similar task before.
Sometimes even to live is an act of courage.
Information learned in a particular mood is often recalled more easily later while in that same mood.
Small, gradual changes to a routine are more likely to become lasting habits than large, sudden overhauls.
Divide each difficulty into as many parts as is feasible and necessary to resolve it.
People tend to seek out information that confirms what they already believe, and avoid what might contradict it.
It is not enough to have a good mind; the main thing is to use it well.
Watching someone else perform an action activates some of the same brain circuits as performing it yourself.
The presence of a single bystander reduces the odds that any one person will help in an emergency, and more bystanders reduce it further.
Fear cannot be without hope nor hope without fear.
Simply naming an emotion out loud can measurably calm the brain's threat-response regions.
The greatest pride, or the greatest despondency, is the greatest ignorance of one's self.
People are more likely to help a stranger right after they've experienced a small stroke of good luck themselves.
Changing the default option on a form can dramatically change how many people choose it, without changing the options themselves.
Two things fill the mind with ever new and increasing admiration and awe: the starry heavens above me and the moral law within me.
The brain treats social exclusion as a genuine threat, triggering stress responses similar to physical danger.
He who is cruel to animals becomes hard also in his dealings with men.
People tend to believe, after the fact, that an event was more predictable than it actually was.
Comparing yourself to people slightly better off tends to lower satisfaction, while comparing to people slightly worse off tends to raise it.
He who has a why to live can bear almost any how.
Children typically develop a working understanding of other people's beliefs and perspectives around age four.
There is always some madness in love. But there is also always some reason in madness.
Simply expecting a task to be difficult can change how much effort and attention we actually put into it.
People are more persuaded by an argument when they believe they arrived at the reasoning themselves.
One must still have chaos in oneself to be able to give birth to a dancing star.
Chronic stress can shrink the hippocampus, the brain region central to forming new memories.
Without music, life would be a mistake.
The brain consolidates and strengthens new memories primarily during deep sleep, not while awake.
People tend to behave more honestly when there's a mirror in the room, even without realizing why.
Talent hits a target no one else can hit; genius hits a target no one else can see.
source: The World as Will and Representation

text: Even brief exercise can measurably improve mood for hours afterward.
The two enemies of human happiness are pain and boredom.
First impressions of a person's face form within a tenth of a second, before conscious thought has a chance to catch up.
Delaying gratification in childhood correlates with a range of positive outcomes later in life, though later research shows a stable home environment matters just as much as willpower.
Life can only be understood backwards; but it must be lived forwards.
People tend to feel closer to a stranger after a structured series of increasingly personal questions exchanged between them.
Anxiety is the dizziness of freedom.
The brain's reward system often responds more strongly to anticipating a reward than to actually receiving it.
Listing three things you're grateful for before bed is linked to measurably better sleep quality.
Reason is, and ought only to be the slave of the passions.
We tend to judge how common or important something is by how easily examples come to mind. This is the availability heuristic.
A wise man proportions his belief to the evidence.
No man's knowledge here can go beyond his experience.
It is better to be a human being dissatisfied than a pig satisfied.
He who knows only his own side of the case knows little of that.
Doubt is not a pleasant condition, but certainty is absurd.
Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains.
Patience is bitter, but its fruit is sweet.
All of humanity's problems stem from man's inability to sit quietly in a room alone.
The heart has its reasons which reason knows nothing of.
The greatest thing in the world is to know how to belong to oneself.
He who fears he shall suffer, already suffers what he fears.
Do every act of your life as if it were your last.
It is not death that a man should fear, but he should fear never beginning to live.
While we wait for life, life passes.
Seek not that the things which happen should happen as you wish; but wish the things which happen to be as they are.
Writing briefly about your core values before a stressful test is linked to better performance on it.
Our sense of how fast time passes seems to speed up with age, partly because each year is a smaller fraction of the life we've lived so far.
Even a forced smile can slightly lift mood, through feedback from facial muscles to the brain.
People tend to trust information more when it's presented in a font that's easy to read.
The brain can form a snap judgment about a stranger's trustworthiness in under a tenth of a second.
Repeating a false claim, even to debunk it, can make it feel more familiar and therefore more believable later.
People tend to feel more pain from an injury they believe was caused deliberately than one caused by accident.
A short period of boredom often boosts creative thinking immediately afterward.
A supportive touch, like a hand on the shoulder, can measurably lower stress hormone levels.
People consistently underestimate how much their personality and preferences will change in the future, even while acknowledging how much they've already changed.
Choice overload can be reduced simply by grouping similar options into clearly labeled categories.
A short walk in a natural setting restores attention and reduces mental fatigue more effectively than a walk through a city.
People tend to make riskier choices when a potential loss is framed as avoidable, compared to when the same outcome is framed as a gain forfeited.
Two people who feel socially close to each other show more synchronized brain activity while having a conversation.
Believing you have more control over a random event, like rolling dice yourself instead of watching someone else, can make you feel more confident about the outcome.
People tend to eat more food when eating in a group than when eating alone, regardless of hunger level.
A brief period of physical discomfort, like holding an uncomfortable position, can measurably increase feelings of moral seriousness in a decision made right after.
Deciding in advance exactly when and where you'll do something makes you significantly more likely to follow through, a technique called an implementation intention.
People are more likely to comply with a large request after first agreeing to a smaller, related one.
People are sometimes more likely to agree to a smaller request after first refusing a larger one.
The brain's stress response can be measurably reduced just by reframing anxiety as excitement before a challenging task.
People who feel awe, such as looking at a vast landscape, tend to report feeling like time is more abundant afterward.
Simply looking at images of nature, even in photographs, can modestly reduce stress compared to looking at urban scenes.
The brain updates its beliefs faster in response to good news than to bad news of equal size, a pattern called the optimism bias in learning.
The whole is more than the sum of its parts.
Man is by nature a political animal.
While there's life, there's hope.
The life of the dead is placed in the memory of the living.
Knowledge is power.
Reading maketh a full man; conference a ready man; and writing an exact man.
Covenants, without the sword, are but words.
The owl of Minerva spreads its wings only with the falling of the dusk.
The only way to have a friend is to be one.
The mass of men lead lives of quiet desperation.
Go confidently in the direction of your dreams. Live the life you've imagined.
By failing to prepare, you are preparing to fail.
An investment in knowledge pays the best interest.
He suffers more than necessary, who suffers before it is necessary.
Associate with people who are likely to improve you.
Reading furnishes the mind only with materials of knowledge; it is thinking that makes what we read ours.
Beauty in things exists in the mind which contemplates them.
The only freedom which deserves the name is that of pursuing our own good in our own way.
A pupil from whom nothing is ever demanded which he cannot do, never does all he can.
Common sense is not so common.
To renounce liberty is to renounce being a man.
The last thing one knows in constructing a work is what to put first.
I quote others only to better express myself.
First learn the meaning of what you say, and then speak.
